coredata.dc:title: pH-responsive and sustained release drug delivery system of BSA coated CDs-DOX
coredata.dc:creator: Duan, Qianqian
Shi, Jiaying
Zhou, Lan
Zhang, Boye
Wang, Xiaoyuan
Sang, Shengbo
coredata.dc:description:
Background The drugs are easily cleared and lack of targeting in chemotherapy, thus reducing its efficacy. A novel pH- responsive drug delivery system based on carbon quantum dots (CDs) was proposed. CDs have low toxicity and good biocompatibility and can be electrically combined with doxorubicin (DOX). The bovine serum albumin (BSA), an endogenousx substance, could be used as a protein model for constructing a novel lipoprotein-like nanocarrier. In this study, we introduced BSA into the drug delivery system and prepared a stable drug delivery system BSA@CDs-DOX. The system was characterized by UV-Vis, fourier infrared spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy, and its stability, drug loading efficiency, coating efficiency and drug release in vitro were investigated. The release of drug from the system was tracked by the fluorescence properties of CDs.
Results The stability of the BSA@CDs-DOX system was obviously enhanced in various environments. The in vitro release profile was prolonged, and the cumulative release ratio at pH = 5.0 was 7.5 times higher than that at pH = 7.4. The drug side effects on the normal cells were significantly reduced and the killing effect on cancerous cells was improved in longer time. Excellent and sustained migration resistance was observed in 72 h. The drug uptake process and cellular state were evaluated visually in real time until 72 h.
Conclusion The stable and sustained release BSA@CDs-DOX system showed good application prospect in chemotherapy of tumors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: BSA@CDs-DOX
Drug delivery
System stability
pH response

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Study on in vitro encapsulation efficiency of BSA@CDs-DOX
Study on the stability of BSA@CDs-DOX system
In vitro cytotoxicity assay of the system of BSA@CDs-DOX complex
Cell imaging experiment of drug-carrying system
Study on anti-metastasis ability of drug system

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51975400 , 51622507 , 51505324 ), Open Fund from the Key Laboratory of Cellular Physiology (Shanxi Medical University) (No. KLMEC/SXMU202001), Shanxi “1331 Project” Key Subjects Construction ( 1331KSC ), and Basic Research Program of Shanxi for Youths ( 201701D221111 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CDs. The upper left illustration is the particle size distribution images of CDs; (B) TEM image of BSA@CDs-DOX. The illustration is size changes of BSA@CDs-DOX during 12 days; (C) UV-Vis absorption spectra of CDs, DOX, CDs-DOX, BSA@CDs-DOX. The illustration is the fluorescence diagram of CDs, DOX, CDs-DOX, BSA@CDs-DOX under 410 nm excitation; (D) FTIR spectra of CDs, CDs-DOX and BSA@CDs-DOX.

Comparison of the fluorescence stability of BSA@CDs-DOX and CDs-DOX under different conditions: in PBS solution with 0% (A), 5% (B) and 10% (C) FBS volume ratios, and in PRMI 1640 (10% FBS) solution (D). Y0 is the initial fluorescence intensity, and Y is the corresponding value under different times. (The excitation wavelength is 430 nm).
In vitro drug release of CDs-DOX and BSA@CDs-DOX systems in PBS solution. (The solid line through each set of points correspond to the first-order kinetics fitting).

Cell viability of GES-1 and MGC-803 cells incubated with different concentrations of CDs for 24 h and 48 h (A). Cell viability of GES-1 cells (B) and MGC-803 cells (C) incubated with different concentrations of DOX or BSA@CDs-DOX for 24 h and 48 h. (D) Cell viability of MGC-803 cells incubated with different concentrations of CDs-DOX and BSA@CDs-DOX for 24 h and 48 h.

(A) Comparison images of scratches of MGC-803 cells co-incubated with DOX and BSA@CDs-DOX systems for 0 h, 24 h, 48 h and 72 h (scale: 20 µm); (B) The corresponding cell mobility after co-incubation of DOX and BSA@CDs-DOX systems.
The drug tracking capability of fluorescent BSA@CDs-DOX system.
Formation diagram of BSA@CDs-DOX system and drug release process diagram in vivo.

head.ce:title: pH-responsive and sustained release drug delivery system of BSA coated CDs-DOX

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MicroNano System Research Center, College of Information and Computer & Key Laboratory of Advanced Transducers and Intelligent Control System of Ministry of Education and Shanxi Province, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Information and Computer & Key Laboratory of Advanced Transducers and Intelligent Control System of Ministry of Education and Shanxi Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aMicroNano System Research Center, College of Information and Computer & Key Laboratory of Advanced Transducers and Intelligent Control System of Ministry of Education and Shanxi Province, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key laboratory of Cellular Physiology at Shanxi Medical University, Ministry of Education, Taiyuan 030024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key laboratory of Cellular Physiology at Shanxi Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bKey laboratory of Cellular Physiology at Shanxi Medical University, Ministry of Education, Taiyuan 030024, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The drugs are easily cleared and lack of targeting in chemotherapy, thus reducing its efficacy. A novel pH- responsive drug delivery system based on carbon quantum dots (CDs) was proposed. CDs have low toxicity and good biocompatibility and can be electrically combined with doxorubicin (DOX). The bovine serum albumin (BSA), an endogenousx substance, could be used as a protein model for constructing a novel lipoprotein-like nanocarrier. In this study, we introduced BSA into the drug delivery system and prepared a stable drug delivery system BSA@CDs-DOX. The system was characterized by UV-Vis, fourier infrared spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy, and its stability, drug loading efficiency, coating efficiency and drug release in vitro were investigated. The release of drug from the system was tracked by the fluorescence properties of CDs.

The stability of the BSA@CDs-DOX system was obviously enhanced in various environments. The in vitro release profile was prolonged, and the cumulative release ratio at pH = 5.0 was 7.5 times higher than that at pH = 7.4. The drug side effects on the normal cells were significantly reduced and the killing effect on cancerous cells was improved in longer time. Excellent and sustained migration resistance was observed in 72 h. The drug uptake process and cellular state were evaluated visually in real time until 72 h.
The stable and sustained release BSA@CDs-DOX system showed good application prospect in chemotherapy of tumors.

A fluorescent drug-loading system BSA@CDs-DOX with high stability and extended drug circulation time was successfully prepared, which could be used for real-time tracking of drugs and treatment effect.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:section.ce:para: Chemotherapy is currently one of the most frequently used methods in clinical practice. Chemotherapeutic drugs are usually directly transported into the body through blood vessels, which likely results in drug accumulation in the blood circulation, and causes systemic toxic and side effects [1]. In addition, the drug metabolism is fast and lacks specific recognition, thus reducing the therapeutic effect of the drug. To solve the above problems, the new multifunctional drug delivery systems with extended release cycle time were proposed.

With the continuous development of medical technology and nanotechnology, carbon quantum dots (CDs) have attracted much attention in the field of drug delivery and fluorescent tracing [2,3]. The CDs have unique advantages such as luminescence properties, biocompatibility, water solubility, and low toxicity [4–6]. However, due to the small size of CDs, they are easily cleared by plasma, and result in a short cycle time and fast body metabolism. The structural stability of the drug delivery system based on CDs is an important prerequisite to prevent excessive premature drug leakage before entering the tumor site, and then decrease the side-effect to normal organs and enhance the tumor inhibition efficiency [6]. The bovine serum albumin (BSA), an endogenous substance, could be used as a protein model for constructing a novel lipoprotein-like nanocarrier [7]. The BSA coating can prolong the circulation time of nanocarriers in vivo, and target tumor cells through GP60 receptor and SPARC (acid secreting protein of transformed cysteine) mediated endocytosis, increasing the local concentration of drugs in tumor tissues [8–11].

In this paper, the drug doxorubicin (DOX) and CDs were combined by electrostatic interaction, and then the BSA was used to coat to form BSA@CDs-DOX system (Scheme 1 ). The BSA@CDs-DOX system could avoid the recognition of reticuloendothelial (RES) tissue in vivo and penetrate into blood vessels through the EPR (enhanced permeability and retention) effect in tumor tissues [12–15]. The drug delivery system was pH responsive, and the structure would be broken under the faintly acidic environments of tumor tissue, then the drug DOX on the BSA peptide chain was released continuously and steadily. Here, the stability, cytotoxicity, cycle time extension, cell imaging and migration of the system were studied to evaluate the drug delivery efficiency of BSA@CDs-DOX.

Urea, citric acid, doxorubicin hydrochloride, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were purchased from China National Pharmaceutical Holdings Co., Ltd. Dialysis bag (MD34), phosphate buffered saline (PBS), Methylthiazolyldiphenyl - tetrazolium bromide (MTT) were purchased from Guangzhou Saiguo Biological Technology Co., Ltd. Fetal bovine serum (FBS), cell culture fluid (RPMI 1640), 4% paraformaldehyde, and sterile slides were purchased from Mike Ward Biology Co., Ltd, China. The above chemicals were analytical reagents and used without further purification. All experimental deionized water was obtained through the Milli-Q water system (Shanghai Yi heng Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd).

The morphology of samples was characterized by high resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL2100F, Nippon Electronics). The surface functional groups of the samples were tested using fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Tensor 27, Bruker Optik, Germany). The absorption spectra of the samples were determined by UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Metash UV-8000A, 190 nm-1200 nm, China). Fluorescence spectra of samples were measured by FLS-980 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). Cell viability (OD value) was obtained by MTT assay using Microplate Reader (Thermo Scientific, Multiskan FC, China). Cell imaging results were carried out by fluorescence microscopy (Leica Microsystems, Leica DM 3000, China).

Firstly, 1 g of citric acid and 2 g of urea were dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water, and the mixture was placed in a microwave oven and heated at 720 W for 5 min to obtain yellow-brown raw carbon quantum dot. The prepared CDs was dissolved in a small amount of deionized water and centrifuged (6000 r/min, 7 min) to remove the solid precipitate to obtain the supernatant. Finally, the supernatant was placed in a dialysis bag and dialyzed in deionized water for 24 h. After that, the supernatant was transferred to a real air drying oven for one day (60 °C) to obtain solid pure CDs.

Then 0.1 mg/mL CDs solution was mixed with 0.1 mg/mL DOX solution at the same volume, and the pH of the mixture was adjusted to 7.4 through PBS buffer. The mixed solution was stirred overnight at 37 °C in dark conditions. Finally, the mixture was centrifuged (6000 r/min, 5 min) and dialysed (3500 Da) for 24 h. The obtained CDs-DOX solution was put in a freeze dryer to remove the solvent. Then add 5 mL of CDs-DOX (solution prepared in DMSO) into 5 mL of BSA with different concentrations (0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.07, 0.1, 0.15, 0.2 mg/mL, solution of deionized water), and stir for 5 minutes to form the nanoparticles of BSA@CDs-DOX. Then the BSA@CDs-DOX was precipitated by salt precipitation and freeze-dried to remove DMSO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on in vitro encapsulation efficiency of BSA@CDs-DOX
ce:section.ce:para:
The prepared CDs-DOX solution contains free DOX, which is removed by dialysis method to obtain pure CDs-DOX. The drug loading efficiency formula is as follows:
drug
loading
efficiency
(
wt %
)
=
m 0
/
m 1
×
100 % .
Where m0 is the total amount of the linking drug; m1 is total amount of nanomedicine.

The prepared BSA@CDs-DOX solution also contains free BSA, free CDs-DOX. Salt precipitation was used to precipitate all free proteins and protein nanoparticles. The supernatant was taken and detected by ultraviolet absorption method to calculate the amount of free CDs-DOX. The formula used to calculate the coverage efficiency is:
coverage
efficiency
( % )
=
(
A 1
−
A 0
)
/
A 1
×
100 % .
Where A0 is the amount of CDs-DOX in the supernatant; A1 is the total amount of CDs-DOX. The high encapsulation solution was then placed in the freeze-dryer to remove the DMSO solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the stability of BSA@CDs-DOX system
ce:section.ce:para: CDs-DOX and BSA@CDs-DOX systems were dispersed in PBS solution (containing 0%, 5% and 10% volume ratios of FBS, pH = 7.4) and cell culture medium PRMI 1640 (containing 10% FBS), respectively. Then their fluorescence intensities were detected at different times. Lastly the fluorescence intensities were compared to evaluate the stability of the systems.

10 mL of BSA@CDs-DOX solution (0.1 mg/mL) was placed in a dialysis bag. The buffer solutions with different pH values (7.4 and 5.0) were placed in 100 mL beakers and stirred at 37 °C in dark. 2 mL of the solution was taken from the beaker at different time intervals, and then 2 mL of the buffer solution was added. The drug release experiment was continued 24 h or 48 h. The absorbance value (at 480 nm) of the extracted solution was measured, and the cumulative release rate of the drug at different times was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity assay of the system of BSA@CDs-DOX complex
ce:section.ce:para: MGC-803 cells (human gastric cancer) were used as experimental models. The cytotoxicity test was carried out by MTT method. The cells were incubated in a humidified 5% CO2 incubator at 37°C. After 80% of the cells were attached to the bottom of the hole, 150 µL of BSA@CD-DOX solutions with different concentrations were added to each plate and co-incubated for 24 h, 48 h or 60 h. Untreated cells were used as a control group. Then, 10 µL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was added to each well. After being kept in the incubator for 4 h, the well plate was taken out and the supernatant was removed. The formation crystals were taken up in wells with 100 µL of DMSO solution. Finally, the optical density (OD) value of each well at 450 nm was measured by a microplate reader, and the cell activity rate was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell imaging experiment of drug-carrying system
ce:section.ce:para: After the MGC-803 cells incubated in 24-well plate for 24 h, 1 mL of culture solution (BSA@CDs-DOX (30 µL)) was added and then co-incubated for different times (0.5, 2, 4, 6, 24, 48, 72 h). Next the cells were washed three times with PBS, and 1 mL of paraformaldehyde (mass fraction of 4%) was added to each hole. After 10 min, the climb piece was removed on the glass. Fluorescence imaging of the cells in different stages were observed by fluorescence microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on anti-metastasis ability of drug system
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the metastatic ability of cancer cells, scratch test was performed. Succinctly, the cell layer was scratched with the tip of the sterile pipette when MGC-803 cells (grown in a 12-well plate) approach aggregation. Cells were then incubated with DOX (0.789 µg/mL) or BSA@CDs-DOX (0.96 µg/mL) for fixed hours (0, 24, 48, 72 h) and photographed for observation. Untreated cells served as a control.

The particle size and distribution of the carrier are important factors that affect its physical stability and distribution in the body. Nanoparticles smaller than 200 nm could gather at the tumor site by EPR effect to achieve a long-term circulation [16,17]. As can be seen from Fig. 1 A-1B, the CDs were uniform dispersion with an average size of 2.5 nm. After loading of DOX and BSA coating, the size of BSA@CDs-DOX increased to 5 nm, and still maintained good dispersion. The size of BSA@CDs-DOX system almost had no change after 12 days, and the favorable stability may be due to the electrostatic repulsion and bit group effect of CDs [18].

According to the absorption spectra (Fig. 1C), the above systems showed strong absorption in ultraviolet and visible regions, which was consistent with the literature report [19]. The strong absorption peaks of CDs at 242 nm were originated from the conjugated structure of the C-H bond. The absorption peaks of CDs at 410 nm were probably related to the π electron transition of oxygen-containing CDs, which is caused by the " n→π" transition of C = O bond [20]. The CDs-DOX system appeared a new absorption peak at 484 nm, corresponding to the characteristic peak of DOX. Compared with CDs-DOX, the visible region absorption peak of BSA@CDs-DOX system was weaker, which may result from the coating of protein BSA [21]. The change of the absorption spectra indicated the formation of the BSA@CDs-DOX system. The fluorescence spectra of CDs-DOX and BSA@CDs-DOX systems were almost consistent with the spectrum of bare CDs (the inset of Fig. 1C). The excitation wavelength dependence was observed and the fluorescent intensity was maximum at 430 nm excitation (Fig. S1). The excellent fluorescent property makes them possible to track the drug release process by multichannel imaging.

The surface groups of the systems were characterized by fourier infrared spectrometer (FTIR spectra), as shown in Fig. 1D. The broadband absorption at 3678∼3841 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of O-H or N-H bond [20,22–24]. The peak near 1687 cm−1 was caused by -COO-, indicating the presence of carboxyl groups [18]. Thus the CDs was rich in carboxyl groups. New peak of 2922 cm−1 appeared in CDs-DOX system, which resulted from the stretching vibration of C-H bonds [21]. After coating of BSA, BSA@CDs-DOX system had a wide absorption peak around 3460 cm−1, which may be caused by multi-molecule association, and stretching vibration of O-H bond and N-H bond [25–27]. The peaks at 1418 cm−1 and 1653 cm−1 indicated the existence of CDs-DOX [28]. Meanwhile, the sharp characteristic peak at 1032 cm−1 indicated the existence of BSA. The above comparison indicated that the CDs-DOX drug-loading system had been successfully encapsulated in BSA, forming the BSA@CDs-DOX nanostructure. The optimized drug-loading efficiency of BSA@CDs-DOX systems was 72.9% (Fig. S2).

The system stability was evaluated by the fluorescence changes in different culture media containing FBS [29]. The fluorescent stability of the systems was first studied in PBS solutions containing 0%, 5% and 10% FBS for 50 min, respectively [30]. As shown in Fig. 2 A, both the CDs-DOX and BSA@CDs-DOX were relatively stable, and the difference of the stability was small. However, the obvious difference was observed after FBS added. The fluorescent intensities of CDs-DOX system obviously increased with the concentration of FBS, indicating the DOX fell off from the system along with time. The relatively poor stability of CDs-DOX was significantly improved by BSA coating. The BSA@CDs-DOX system showed better stability than CDs-DOX (Fig. 2B-2C), and especially the fluorescence of BSA@CDs-DOX almost had no changes in PBS solution with 10% FBS. The superior stability of BSA@CDs-DOX system was also observed in the actual cell culture of PRMI 1640 solution containing 10% FBS. Fig. 2D showed that within the first 30 min, both systems released certain drugs, but the release amount of BSA@CDs-DOX was significantly lower. After 1h, the drug release of BSA@CDs-DOX was stable, and the release of the drug DOX was maintained at a fixed quantity. While the drug release of CDs-DOX continued to rise, which led to premature and quick leak of drug. Therefore, it could be inferred that the BSA coating is beneficial to prolong the circulation time and drug release time until reached the tumor site, and the BSA@CDs-DOX system is expected to obtain a sustained drug release effect.

The sustained release property is essential for an excellent drug delivery system. BSA could act as the gatekeeper to prevent the rapid release of drugs [31], and its effect on drug release behavior in PBS solution was studied. As can be seen from Fig. 3 , the cumulative release of DOX at pH = 5.0 was significantly higher than that at pH = 7.4 for both the CDs-DOX and BSA@CDs-DOX systems, which demonstrated the pH-responsive property of the two systems. Under acidic condition, the hydrophobic bond between BSA and CDs-DOX was opened, and CDs-DOX fell off. Then the electrostatic force between CDs-DOX was protonated, and DOX was released from the carrier [14,32]. At pH = 5.0, the maximum cumulative released ratios of the two systems were comparable. At pH = 7.4, the cumulative released ratio of BSA@CDs-DOX was about half of CDs-DOX. The rate of the cumulative released ratio at pH = 5.0 to pH = 7.4 increased from 4 to 7.5 after BSA coating. The amplified release distinction between pH = 5.0 and 7.4 is beneficial to enhance the target ability and decrease the side-effect to normal cells [33].

In addition, the first-order kinetic fitting analysis of drug release curves showed that the release rate constants of CDs-DOX were 0.842 and 0.53, and the release rate constants of BSA@CDs-DOX were 0.206 and 0.106, respectively, under the conditions of pH = 7.4 and 5.0. It can be confirmed that the release rate of the two drug delivery systems is fast at pH = 5.0 [34]. Moreover, under the condition of pH = 5.0, it was obvious that the maximum release amount of CDs-DOX was 12 h, while the release amount of BSA@CDs-DOX was about 38 h.

Hence, the release rate of BSA@CDs-DOX was relatively slow. Thus, in the blood environment, BSA@CDs-DOX drug delivery system makes DOX stay in the system longer and reduces the non-specific toxic and side effects of DOX. When the system enters cancer cells, most drugs are released at pH = 5.0.

The biotoxicity of BSA@CDs-DOX drug delivery systems was studied by using the MGC-803 and GES-1 cells as models. For both the normal cells GES-1 and cancerous cells MGC-803, the biotoxicity of CDs was very low even at high-dose (Fig. 4 A). As shown in Fig. 4B, for the normal cells GES-1, the biotoxicity of drug-loading system BSA@CDs-DOX was obviously lower than that of the bare DOX. The cell viability rates were still about 81.29% and 78.1% after co-incubation with a high drug concentration of 14.56 µg/mL for 24 h and 48 h, and the viability was relatively stable with different concentrations. While the viability of cells treated with bare DOX was only 40.61 % and 32.77% with the same concentration. The results demonstrated that the BSA coating system effectively reduced the side-effects to the normal cells. For the cancerous cells, the dose and time dependent death property was observed for both DOX and BSA@CDs-DOX system, as shown in Fig. 4C. The cell toxicity of BSA@CDs-DOX system was a little lower than DOX in 48 h, but higher in longer time of 60 h. The bare DOX had no selectivity and it was more direct to the cells [35]. While the BSA had specific recognition and could selectively target cancer cells by the overexpressed SPARC on tumor surface, promoting the accumulation of drug-loaded albumin nanoparticles in the tumor stroma. And the pH-responsive property of BSA@CDs-DOX system was verified again, the system was relatively stable in neutral environment of normal cells and only a small amount of drugs were released. In the weak acid environment of cancerous cells, the drug DOX was released and showed cell toxicity.

In addition, the sustained release property of BSA@CDs-DOX system was also noticed when the incubation time extended to 60 h, as shown in Fig. 4C, under the low concentration (<3.64 µg/mL), the killing effect of BSA@CDs-DOX was comparable to that of DOX. The low dosage may be completely released in the incubation time, and the difference of the two drug systems was difficult to reflect. Under the higher concentration (7.28 µg/mL, 14.56 µg/mL), the cell viability of BSA@CDs-DOX group was higher than that of DOX for 24 h and 48 h, while it was obviously reduced for 60 h. The sustained release property of BSA@CDs-DOX system was clearly manifested for the higher drug concentration. With the incubation time 24 h, 48 h, and 60 h, the corresponding cell survival rates dropped to 60%, 40% and 19%, and the corresponding IC50 values were 3.4 µg/mL and 2.83 µg/mL for 48 h and 60 h, respectively (Fig. S3).

The migration of tumor cells is one of the most important features of malignancy. In different time intervals, the change of the width of the uncovered area was observed to quantify the effect of cell migration, and then the slow release control performance of the drug delivery system BSA@CDs-DOX was proved [36,37]. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the migration effect of cells without drug treatment (control group) was slight after 24 h incubation, while the obvious basic fusion was observed at 48 h and 72 h with a calculated mobility rate as high as 74.5% and 94%. Both DOX and BSA@CDs-DOX system had inhibitory effects on cell migration. The migration pattern of cells treated with DOX was similar to that of the control group, but its migration ability was weakened. The DOX exhibited a favourable anti-migration ability at 24 h, but with the extension of time, the migration rate rebounded to 43% and 79% at 48 h and 72 h. For the BSA@CDs-DOX treatment group, the cell migration ability was continuously inhibited in 72 h, and the cell migration rate was reduced to 10%. These results showed that BSA-coated CDs-DOX effectively inhibited cell migration, and the anti-migration effect was preferable to that of other groups. The inhibition effect of drug delivery system on cell activity and migration ability is the key to prevent tumor metastasis. The excellent performance of BSA@CDs-DOX system may result from sustained slow release of DOX through the BSA coating, thus inhibiting cell metastasis [38].

The cell uptake of BSA@CDs-DOX was evaluated visually by incubation with MGC-803 cells for different periods [39]. The green fluorescence of CDs and red fluorescence of DOX could be detected at 30 min, and the CDs and DOX were mainly located in cytoplasm in 4 h (Fig. 6 ). As time went by, bright red and green fluorescence was observed both in the nucleus and cytoplasm at 6 h, indicating that DOX and CDs entered the cells. The red fluorescence was observed in the nucleus of cells treated by CDs-DOX at only 1 h [40]. The coating of BSA slowed down the release of DOX and improved the stability of the system in the medium. The increase of the fluorescence intensity of the drug in the cells was observed at 24 h, indicating that BSA@CDs-DOX was effectively internalized into the MGC-803 cells [41]. The cell contraction was observed at 72 h, manifesting the killing effect of the system on the cells. These results showed that the BSA@CDs-DOX system could realize drug tracking visually in real time.

The slow released BSA@CDs-DOX drug loading system was prepared using a very facile method. The BSA coating made the system more stable to prolong the drug circulation time. The cumulative release of BSA@CDs-DOX system at pH = 5.0 was 7.5 times higher than that at pH = 7.4. The pH-responsive and targeted performance significantly reduced the side effects on the normal cells. The sustained release performance enhanced the killing and migration inhibition effects on the cancerous cells in longer time. The inherent fluorescence drug loading system was convenient to conduct the real-time tracking of the drug and its therapeutic effects. Therefore, the multifunctional BSA@CDs-DOX system showed promising prospect of application in drug sustained-release and tracking.

Qianqian Duan: Conceptualization, Methodology. Jiaying Shi: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Lan Zhou: Visualization. Boye Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Xiaoyuan Wang: Writing – review & editing. Shengbo Sang: Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51975400, 51622507, 51505324), Open Fund from the Key Laboratory of Cellular Physiology (Shanxi Medical University) (No. KLMEC/SXMU202001), Shanxi “1331 Project” Key Subjects Construction (1331KSC), and Basic Research Program of Shanxi for Youths (201701D221111).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2021.131358.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Doxorubicin-loaded fluorescent carbon dots with PEI passivation as a drug delivery system for cancer therapy
Nanoscale

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Hailing, L. Xiufang, W. Lili, L. Baoqiang, H. Kaichen, H. Yongquan, Z. Qianqian, M. Chaoming, R. Xiaoshuai, Z. Rui, L. Hui, P. Pengfei, S. Hong, Doxorubicin-loaded fluorescent carbon dots with PEI passivation as a drug delivery system for cancer therapy, Nanoscale. 12 (2020) 17222–17237. https://doi.org/10.1039/D0NR01236J.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Endocytosis in cellular uptake of drug delivery vectors: Molecular aspects in drug development
Bioorg. Med. Chem.

Dąbrowska, Endocytosis in cellular uptake of drug delivery vectors: Molecular aspects in drug development, Bioorg. Med. Chem.28 (2020) 115556. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bmc.2020.115556.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent carbon dots are the new quantum dots: an overview of their potential in emerging technologies and nanosafety
J. Mater. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.O. Alas, F.B. Alkas, A. Aktas Sukuroglu, R. Genc Alturk, D. Battal, Fluorescent carbon dots are the new quantum dots: an overview of their potential in emerging technologies and nanosafety, J. Mater.
